Name: RichardCapricorn
Verbatim Review: I've been using DL for over 3 years. I initially started using it to get Fluent in Spanish and reached a high enough level to use it on vacation. My focus switched to Portuguese as primary language because I want to move to Brazil. Gamification only matters to me as a means to sort of track consistency. I care more about the score rather than the XP. I am using other resources such as books and other apps
Review Summary: 3+ year user who hit a functional ceiling in Spanish and shifted to Portuguese for real-world goals; supplements Duolingo with books and other apps.
Key Quote: I care more about the score rather than the XP

Name: SweetPeasAreNice
Verbatim Review: My streak is about 2600 days. I've gone from zero to 'can read pretty well and follow slow speech' in French and Spanish, about two years each. This for travel. I've gone from 'studied it at school' to 'ready to translate for my family on our upcoming trip' in Japanese. And I've done a few Latin lessons and music lessons for the hell of it. It's a habit now, just as strong as brushing my teeth. A little learning every day. I rarely do more than three lessons a day. I never skip a day. Edit to add: like the other responder, I don't expect to get fluent through Duo, it's to stretch my brain. Like doing Wordle and Connections every day.
Review Summary: 2600-day streak user who treats Duolingo as a daily brain-stretching habit like Wordle; uses it for travel prep and cognitive engagement, not fluency.
Key Quote: I don't expect to get fluent through Duo, it's to stretch my brain

Name: readzalot1
Verbatim Review: I started French during the pandemic to stretch my brain. (And to become a better Canadian). I recently finished the course but I continue by doing the practice lessons daily. I listen to the French radio station, some YouTube and some easy kids books.
Review Summary: Started French during pandemic for brain exercise; finished the course and now supplements with French radio, YouTube, and easy reading.
Key Quote: I started French during the pandemic to stretch my brain

Name: Choice-Ganache6287
Verbatim Review: I'm 66 & have been doing Duo for over 5 years. I definitely do it to exercise my brain. I'm tickled when I remember a new word days after I was introduced to it. It tells me my brain is still functioning and learning, even though I occasionally forget why I walked into a room.
Review Summary: 66-year-old, 5+ year user who uses Duolingo purely for brain exercise; delighted by memory retention as a sign of cognitive health.
Key Quote: It tells me my brain is still functioning and learning

Name: moonbeam0007
Verbatim Review: I turned 80 yesterday and have an 887 day streak in Spanish. I don't speak it very well, or understand fast talking, but I read it pretty well. My memory is bad for vocabulary, and the tenses are hard, but that's why I do it, to exercise the brain. And because I think Spanish is a beautiful language.
Review Summary: 80-year-old with 887-day streak who uses Duolingo to exercise the brain and appreciates Spanish aesthetically, not for conversational fluency.
Key Quote: that's why I do it, to exercise the brain

Name: LongjumpingKiwi6962
Verbatim Review: "Only" on a 1050 day streak. I'm learning German because I moved to Germany. I use Duo now as a supplementary source and not as a main language learning source. Initially the few months before I moved to Germany I used it more intensively as a main language acquisition tool. I think it has helped me a lot with vocab and also just hearing what words should sound like. It's terrible for actual grammar learning. I now have weekly tutoring for that. As another poster mentioned - it becomes a habit. So every morning when I drink my coffee, I try to do a lesson or two. I'm almost done with the German course and would likely start with another course once I am done with this.
Review Summary: 1050-day streak; moved to Germany and uses Duolingo for vocab/pronunciation alongside weekly tutoring for grammar — consciously positioned as a supplement.
Key Quote: It's terrible for actual grammar learning. I now have weekly tutoring for that

Name: unsafeideas
Verbatim Review: I used it and stopped using it 3 times over years. Now is my fourth time. I alternated between languages, treating it mostly as a kind of fun passtime. A game if you will. In the meantime, there were minor and major successes. Minor ones: it helped me to refresh my french. I learned to read ukrainian a little and was capable to follow some netflix shows and youtube channels. I already knew similar slavic language, so this was easy. Major ones: It taught me enough spanish to start watching netflix in it. Now I am listening to some spanish podcasts for natives - it has to be well articulated and about limited range of topics, but I still see it as a huge success. And I can follow simple news in german. Not the real ones, cant watch netflix yet (not even with language reactor), but I understand those simple news and am slowly getting there. I did not finished any course. I switched between them or stopped entirely for periods of time. I have no reason to finis or go back to spanish course - i am listening to podcasts and having fun on netflix. It is ok to not finish.
Review Summary: On and off user (4th stint) who treats Duolingo as a fun pastime; achieved real media consumption in Spanish and partial comprehension in Ukrainian and German without finishing any course.
Usage Duration: On and off over several years
Key Quote: It taught me enough spanish to start watching netflix in it

Name: Egg_lizard_hotel
Verbatim Review: I swap to the language of whatever country I'm traveling to next in the hopes of being able to greet folks, order food, ask for help, and do some reading. It was been super helpful, and I feel like locals are happier when you try to speak their language, even if it's messy. I've been able to hold basic conversations in French, German, and Spanish so far!
Review Summary: Travel-motivated user who swaps languages before each trip to handle basic social interactions; reports success holding basic conversations across three languages.
Key Quote: I've been able to hold basic conversations in French, German, and Spanish so far

Name: Big-Vegetable4550
Verbatim Review: My streak is 2146 today. I started Duolingo six years ago when I was offered a job that meant moving to France for three years, so concentrated on learning French for the first four years - but I was working a lot, so hard to do as much studying. I ended up staying in Europe and moving around a lot and then retired two years ago - so then I had a lot more time for studying. My first four years, I would take breaks from French and learn some other languages (my job involved travel to China during COVID and the lovely four week quarantines - locked in a hotel room and don't come out until your quarantine is finished, so lots of time for Duolingo). But then I really concentrated on French only and finished that course to 130. But now i was hooked - I was able to do pretty much anything I wanted in French (including conversation - Duolingo Max made a big difference there). I took up the German course (I had learned some German in my youth, but forgotten much of it). I found it much easier to learn German, having honed my skills with using Duolingo and understanding how I personally learned best. It helped a lot to be using Max for conversations from the beginning and I enjoyed it a lot. I finished the old German course that went to 80 in about 8 months. I wasn't ready to stop, so I took up the Italian course. I had never learned any Italian before, but between understanding how to use Duolingo to learn and the similarities between French and Italian, I will finish the old Italian course after five months of studying (before end of May). I'm really enjoying learning Italian and am amazed at how well it's going. Where I'm living now, I can listen to Italian radio and am finding I can understand much of it. When I finish the Italian course, if the new versions of German and Italian are released (looking less and less likely that they will be before end of this month), I'll either work on one of the other courses I've started (Arabic, Japanese, Chinese, Korean or Swedish), or else I'll delete either the German or Italian courses and do them again until the updates are finally out. I don't care about the gamification, except I use it to consciously motivate myself.
Review Summary: 2146-day streak; started for job relocation to France, achieved full conversational French, and has since completed German and is finishing Italian — a committed long-term multi-language learner.
Language Learned: French, German, Italian, Arabic, Japanese, Chinese, Korean, Swedish
Key Quote: I was able to do pretty much anything I wanted in French (including conversation)

Name: Verineli
Verbatim Review: Well, I'm still not finished with my first course after three years, so I do that. Sometimes I can only do one lesson, sometimes whole unit, sometimes I do more learning from other sources, so Duo is slow going. I used to share time between French and Mandarin, so it slowed me down too. I dropped Mandarin, but added Norwegian. I mostly learn it from a book, but I supplement with Duo.
Review Summary: Casual 3-year user who hasn't finished a first course; splits time across multiple languages at a slow pace, using Duolingo alongside books.
Key Quote: I mostly learn it from a book, but I supplement with Duo

Name: DJBitterbarn
Verbatim Review: I typically go from language to language over long periods until I start to feel comfortable speaking and understanding the language or until I need to start a new one. I'm not looking to get fluent, which is an advantage, but I'm trying to drill exercises until the grammar and structure feels natural. Plus over years of it you get a lot of exposure. It's the long game, so a 4k streak is just a nice way to keep going and that's been a big help in its own right. I'm slowly grinding Greek now, and it's interesting to see how as I'm going through the course things that were complicated before are now easy, and new things are hard but becoming easier. I have used it for travel already. Chinese (which I should really work back into rotation) is something that I took to 'can occasionally pick up written words and can function alone with going out in China'. And I can read and understand some degree of Dutch now.
Review Summary: 4000-day streak; not chasing fluency but drilling for natural grammar feel across many languages; has achieved functional use in Chinese and Dutch, and uses languages for travel.
Key Quote: It's the long game, so a 4k streak is just a nice way to keep going

Name: hacool
Verbatim Review: I focus mostly on German and have also been doing the reverse course, English from German. I finished the course last summer when it ended at Section Five and finally got the update course last week. So now I can continue with that through B2. I also sometimes dabble with other languages. Several years back (before my current streak) I was going to Denmark and the Netherlands. Three weeks before the trip I remembered that I had Duo so I tried to quickly cram some Danish and Dutch. Result? Not remembering which is which when trying to say thanks. Every now and again I will do some Spanish from German with the idea that it might be nice to learn Spanish and to also practice my German at the same time.
Review Summary: Dedicated German learner who also does reverse courses for extra practice; dabbles in other languages for travel but with limited success from last-minute cramming.
Key Quote: Not remembering which is which when trying to say thanks

Name: RobertDeveloper
Verbatim Review: Im on a 2143 streak on one account and 1605 on a other account. I have done the Italian language tree twice, I am on my first run for the Spanish language tree and also do Polish. For me Duolingo is not for learning a language because I don't think you can learn a language this way, it is more like memory training, like solving a crossword puzzle for me.
Review Summary: Runs two accounts with 2143 and 1605 streaks; frames Duolingo explicitly as memory/cognitive training rather than language learning.
Key Quote: it is more like memory training, like solving a crossword puzzle for me

Name: terryturbojr
Verbatim Review: My streak is in the 2300s I think but I've used the app since 2014. It's only in the last year or so that I've done enough to actually make a dent in the language, so 20-30 minutes a day. Prior to that I was just keeping a streak going with a lesson or two a day which will not learn you much.
Review Summary: 2300+ day streak since 2014 who admits that years of minimal daily lessons didn't lead to learning — only in the past year with 20–30 min/day has he made real progress.

Name: ilumassamuli
Verbatim Review: Next week I'll will have a four-year streak. I first studied French for about one year for work reasons, then I studied mostly Spanish for moving to Spain (finished the course), and the last year I've been studying Mandarin for fun.
Review Summary: Four-year streak user who cycles languages based on life circumstances — work, relocation, and personal interest — finishing the Spanish course and now doing Mandarin for fun.

Name: Ok-Muscle-2131
Verbatim Review: I joined to improve my German. After I got to the end of that course, because it originally topped out at level 80, I started learning Hindi - for travel - but it's a very short course - only goes to level 20. So i picked up Spanish, for enjoyment, and because it's a great world language. I'd still been doing the daily refreshers on German most days but now that the German course has been extended that's my main focus. I'll keep my hand in with Spanish but come back to it after I'm finished with German.
Review Summary: Joined for German, hit the course ceiling, pivoted to Hindi (too short) then Spanish; now refocused on German since the course was extended.

Name: Barbara_Katerina
Verbatim Review: My current streak is only a bit over a year, but I joined Duo in 2013 and have been using it on and off ever since. At first I got it to keep up practice in languages I already knew, but it's not much use for that, and especially back then - I was around b2 in the languages I wanted to practice, and Duo went nowhere near that level. I got myself to finish the Spanish course (as it stood around 2016 I guess?), but it was mostly to prove I could, it wasn't all that useful. Then I stopped using it for a while. I came back to learn German from scratch and found out it works much better for learning from scratch than for languages you know, so I stuck with that for about a year before I stopped having the capacity for it. Then some time after that I got into a Chinese fantasy fandom and so started learning Mandarin, but back then it had really bad character learning support so after about a year I moved to a different app, which had a worse motivation system so I quit soon after. Last year when I was in Germany I returned to the German course, refreshed the old lessons and continued, so that's my 450ish streak now, and I added Mandarin again about a month back - had to restart the course because it changed way too much.
Review Summary: On-and-off user since 2013; discovered Duolingo doesn't serve B2+ learners well, but works for learning from scratch; has cycled through Spanish, German, and Mandarin across multiple attempts.
What They Moved On To: Added another app for Mandarin (then returned to Duo)
Key Quote: Duo went nowhere near that level

Name: OkVermicelli2666
Verbatim Review: User (43F) for 11 years, 2237 day streak currently. I am a hobby linguist and use it daily to brush up on Spanish and French. When I plan to travel, I use it to dabble in some basics. I've learned a handful of words in German, Czech, Turkish, and Hawaiian this way. I tried Arabic but it just wasn't taking. I've also played chess, math, and music.
Review Summary: 11-year user and self-described hobby linguist who maintains Spanish/French and dabbles in basics for travel; also uses chess, math, and music features.
Language Learned: Spanish, French, German, Czech, Turkish, Hawaiian, Arabic
Key Quote: I am a hobby linguist and use it daily to brush up on Spanish and French

Name: intolauren
Verbatim Review: Been regularly using duo since 2013 and my streak is over 2500. I started with Spanish and German and did those exclusively for a year or two (I'm a slow ish learner lol). I then spent some time doing temporary hyper-fixations on Korean and Russian and Turkish for a short time, then got really into the Maths section when they added that and ultimately have ended up going back to Spanish again, with the addition of Finnish (as I'm now married to a Finn and spend a lot of time in Finland where a lot of my in-laws/second family only speak very little english). I now alternate between Spanish and Finnish for a week and it's been really helpful for me! I will say that with doing several languages, once you reach a certain point, it is definitely cool to recognise traits and overlaps of those languages when studying another.
Review Summary: 2500+ streak since 2013; cycles between languages based on life circumstances (married into a Finnish family) and interest; now consistently alternating Spanish and Finnish.
Language Learned: Spanish, German, Korean, Russian, Turkish, Finnish
Key Quote: I'm now married to a Finn and spend a lot of time in Finland where a lot of my in-laws only speak very little english

Name: hockeyandquidditch
Verbatim Review: I'm a fellow joined 2013, but my current streak is a bit behind you at 1953, I think because we've seen so many formats and mistake systems (old tree, new tree, path; old hearts, nothing, new hearts, energy) the changes don't seem as impactful as to newer users.
Review Summary: 1953-day streak since 2013; veteran user who has adapted to many format changes and notes they feel less disruptive with experience.
Language Learned: Swedish, Spanish, French, Norwegian, Latin, German

Name: remmyred2
Verbatim Review: when I started in 2014, I finished the spanish course, then moved onto the german, portuguese and italian as I had backgrounds with these languages, then french and dutch. I did spanish a few times as they updated the course, finished it a few times. the courses were a LOT smaller back then. about 5k xp and you were done. I focused exclusively on korean for a few years, made very little progress, then italian for 2 years, made great progress, and currently, I switch out monthly between 6 languages.
Review Summary: Long-term user since 2014 who has finished multiple courses repeatedly; notes very little progress in Korean vs. great progress in Italian; currently rotating 6 languages monthly.
Language Learned: Spanish, German, Portuguese, Italian, French, Dutch, Korean
Key Quote: I focused exclusively on korean for a few years, made very little progress

Name: Clean_Citron_459
Verbatim Review: It is fun, we do several hours, learning atleast 3 languages together a day, it gives us ideas and I have kinda become a connoisseur in this regard ig.
Review Summary: Uses Duolingo socially with others for several hours a day across 3+ languages; frames it as a fun, idea-generating shared activity.
Key Quote: we do several hours, learning atleast 3 languages together a day

Name: swimmimuf
Verbatim Review: for me, i am learning language grammar and vocabulary really really fast. therefore, hanging on one language is a bit too easy for me. i just love diving into different languages. the only thing i try to avoid is learning really close languages at the same time. for that, i try to go up to B2/C1 in the one language and then start the similar one. Right now, i do this for spanish and italian. all the things with leaderboard doesnt interest me, as i am doing that just for me and for no one else. the only thing i do is my streak, because it just helps me to stay consistent in learning.
Review Summary: Fast learner who cycles languages systematically to B2/C1 before starting similar ones; uses streak for consistency but ignores leaderboard gamification.
Key Quote: I try to go up to B2/C1 in the one language and then start the similar one

Name: Downtown_Common5880
Verbatim Review: I use it to study the language in the country of my next international trip, so I've rotated through about 5 languages since 2022. I'm not really trying or expecting to get fluent, I just want to put in some effort to not be a totally useless and clueless tourist when I travel.
Review Summary: Travel-motivated user who rotates through languages before trips since 2022; modest goals of basic tourist functionality, not fluency.
Key Quote: I just want to put in some effort to not be a totally useless and clueless tourist

Name: Same-Mission225
Verbatim Review: My streak is about 820 days. I've mostly done French for a while but also have circled back to Spanish for a refresher on what I learned in school many years ago. My husband does French but also math and chess for fun.
Review Summary: 820-day streak user doing French as primary and Spanish for refreshing school knowledge; husband uses it for math and chess as entertainment.
Key Quote: I've also circled back to Spanish for a refresher on what I learned in school many years ago

Name: Smooth_Development48
Verbatim Review: Most of the time I've studied two languages at the same time. I also study my new language from my previous language as well as from English so that also bulks up the xps. I think in a way it does become easier with each language because I know how I need to study compared to the first language when I was doing a lot of jumping around trying to figure out how to make everything stick.
Review Summary: Studies two languages simultaneously and uses cross-language learning (e.g., new language from previous language) as a method; has developed a consistent personal learning strategy over time.
Key Quote: I know how I need to study compared to the first language

Name: gs16096
Verbatim Review: My streak is a little over 6 years, and I've done and still do a variety of languages on there. I came into the game with good Japanese and some old high school french and spanish. I now speak very good japanese and spanish and decent french, with not bad italian and portuguese. That's mostly from duolingo although I do use a lot of other sources - grammar study outside of duolingo and meetups/traveling for conversation mainly. At this point, I find duolingo a bit boring when studying from English (my native language) and I mainly now do duolingo 'from' Japanese, which is nice, because it helps my Japanese reading (which is very hard). I find Duolingo generally to be a bit easy and slow so I try to 'skip forward' to a level that I find at least a little bit challenging. I mainly do the latin languages 'from' Japanese, and I will do whichever language is most useful at any given time (upcoming holiday/travel, have friends/flatmate in that language, whatever). I consider duolingo to be a pretty shitty tool, if I'm honest, but I have a terrible tendency to use my phone all the time, and I have just tried to convert that over to duolingo, and, given how much I use it, it definitely has helped my languages. But you 100% need to combine it with actual real life conversation and outside-of-app grammar study.
Review Summary: 6-year user who now speaks Japanese, Spanish, and French well; calls Duolingo 'a pretty shitty tool' but credits volume of use and combination with real-world conversation for his results.
What They Moved On To: Added grammar study and meetups/travel for conversation
Key Quote: you 100% need to combine it with actual real life conversation and outside-of-app grammar study

Name: PodiatryVI
Verbatim Review: I finished Haitian on Duolingo, which I already knew. I also finished French, which I learned in childhood and studied in high school. Now I watch a lot of French TV and YouTube and do some reading. I started Spanish, but I'm mostly using Dreaming Spanish for that. I also started Japanese to keep using the app because I have family members on there. Now I'm doing CI Japanese too.
Review Summary: Finished courses in languages already known; now uses French media extensively and has migrated Spanish learning to Dreaming Spanish; doing Japanese partly for social connection with family on the app.
What They Moved On To: Added Dreaming Spanish and CI Japanese resources
Key Quote: I started Spanish, but I'm mostly using Dreaming Spanish for that

Name: Zentransit
Verbatim Review: After completing all 3 existing levels of Pimsleur Spanish many years ago, I finally decided to subscribe to Duolingo. My streak ended at 187 when l finally realized that I was more concerned about the streak than learning Spanish. After some point I hit a plateau and felt that I was mostly just guessing correct answers without knowing the grammatical rules for assessing proper word order. I therefore, added the Babbel subscription to my stable where I would find myself completely immersed into learning Spanish grammar somewhere near 11: 30 pm at night and I'd suddenly stopped my corrective Babbel lesson and returned to Duolingo, just to maintain my stupid streak! That's when I quit Duolingo and temporarily ended my streak. However, I soon experienced a family tragedy, got terribly depressed and quit my interest in Spanish altogether for about 7 months. Seems I'd forgotten so much that I felt the need to return to both Duolingo and Babbel simultaneously, just to restore my memory. I therefore, returned to Duolingo and deleted my history so that I could start from scratch. In doing so, I've discovered that Duolingo has improved immensely in it's ability to instruct grammar and my Spanish language learning is being profoundly reinforced. I'm once again enjoying my Spanish journey with Duolingo!
Review Summary: Realized streak maintenance was overriding actual learning; quit, added Babbel for grammar, left for 7 months after personal tragedy, then returned fresh and found the app had improved enough to re-engage with genuinely.
What They Moved On To: Added Babbel; quit and restarted Duolingo from scratch
Key Quote: I was more concerned about the streak than learning Spanish

Name: WintersInBerlin
Verbatim Review: I have a langue's degree; Duolingo really help with vocab.
Review Summary: Has a languages degree; uses Duolingo specifically for vocabulary reinforcement as a supplement.
Key Quote: I have a langue's degree; Duolingo really help with vocab

Name: Pleasant_Flatworm866
Verbatim Review: I have been on for several years working on several languages, and now chess. I bought a lifetime...what? Membership? Subscription? Plan?--when it was offered cut-rate. I accept Duolingo's limitations and take it for what it is, an easy, fun way to learn at least something about something, and I accept its occasional flaws. Plus I have a crush on Lily. I never really cared for Junior.
Review Summary: Multi-year user with a lifetime plan who has made peace with Duolingo's limitations; uses it casually for languages and chess as enjoyable light entertainment.
Key Quote: I accept Duolingo's limitations and take it for what it is, an easy, fun way to learn at least something about something

Name: naninaninanntoka
Verbatim Review: 2500+ in Japanese here. Apart from occasional bursts of other languages when I need to travel, it's just a great way to make sure get a little daily revision, complementing lessons elsewhere by going over simpler stuff. And for that purpose, for me, it really works.
Review Summary: 2500+ day Japanese learner who uses Duolingo for daily light revision alongside other learning resources; fits travel-specific language bursts around a consistent Japanese practice.
Key Quote: it's just a great way to make sure get a little daily revision, complementing lessons elsewhere

Name: Business_Egg_9340
Verbatim Review: Linguist here. I've been using Duolingo for 14 years, when it was just getting started and an ELL student told me about it. I've dipped into probably every language. I finished a couple (Spanish, French, German for review, others for acquisition), and dip back into languages in progress as the spirit moves me. During the last year I've focused on the Gaelics and Kreyol, but also Chess (the only effective chess tutorial I've ever used) and recently I discovered that they added all the math through Calculus and so I've been going to town on that. I'm a math teacher and I'm always reviewing/reacquiring skills and test-driving platforms to us with my students for independent practice.
Review Summary: Professional linguist and math teacher using Duolingo for 14 years across virtually every language; now using chess and calculus features to evaluate for student use.
Language Learned: Spanish, French, German, Gaelic, Kreyol, others

Name: jacques-vache-23
Verbatim Review: I principly used Duolingo for Spanish, but I thought I'd see what happens if I learn a lot of languages at once. For most I do a lesson a day. It is amazing that one can learn 8 languages at once. I think doing so many activates the parts of the brain that we use to learn our first language as a baby.
Review Summary: Primarily a Spanish learner who branched into 8 simultaneous languages as an experiment; frames multi-language exposure as activating innate language acquisition.

Name: pizza_alta
Verbatim Review: I alternate languages and recently have been learning chess. I don't care about xp and leagues any longer but still care about my streak and monthly quests, it's a useful reminder not to skip lessons.
Review Summary: Language alternator who has disengaged from XP/leagues but uses streak and monthly quests as a habit-keeping reminder.

Name: Vast_Pangolin_2351
Verbatim Review: I've been with Duolingo since 2014. Years ago it wasn't easy getting internet if you were travelling in Europe so my streak is only about 4 years. My main languages are French and Spanish but I have other ones. If I'm travelling to a country I like to try to learn some of the language. It makes a big difference in how they treat you.
Review Summary: User since 2014 with a 4-year streak; focuses on French and Spanish with travel-motivated language dips; motivated by the positive social reception of attempting local languages.

Name: 1XRobot
Verbatim Review: First, I finished the French course. Then, they added more French course. I finished that too. Now, I'm working through the Mandarin course.
Review Summary: Course completionist who finishes each course fully before moving on; has completed French twice (as it expanded) and now working through Mandarin.
Key Quote: First, I finished the French course. Then, they added more French course. I finished that too.

Name: EsbenLandgren
Verbatim Review: Shortly I will reach 4000 days streak. I can read news and comments in about 20 languages, but speaking with natives is still hard for me. I love spending time with the green owl, it's easy dopamine for me.
Review Summary: Approaching 4000-day streak; has built passive reading ability across ~20 languages but struggles with spoken conversation; openly frames Duolingo as 'easy dopamine.'
Key Quote: I love spending time with the green owl, it's easy dopamine for me

Name: ellenkeyne
Verbatim Review: It's absolutely easier to learn languages the more languages you study. I started Duolingo in 2014 to practice my Spanish, German, and Swedish (all of which I'd formally studied in school/college), and then got interested in French, Brazilian Portuguese, and Italian. Back when DL just had the path, I completed all six courses (earning a 'golden owl') and continued to do daily practice in each. I'm B2 or B1 in those six languages. I also just like languages, so I took at least a couple of lessons in everything DL offered from English or Spanish (except the conlangs). I finally got to learn the Greek, Cyrillic, Yiddish/Hebrew, and Arabic writing systems. I finished the brief Latin course because I'd studied that for a year, so why not? I experimented with Ukrainian and Russian because of current events, Haitian Creole and Yiddish to see their relationships with French and German, and Scottish Gaelic for vague family heritage reasons. And I tackled Welsh and Modern Greek in earnest just because they've always interested me. Switching between languages helps me practice the B-level ones I want to maintain, pick up more of my A1 and A2 languages, and complete quests -- because yes, I like gamification. It keeps me practicing daily. No, Duolingo isn't my only language resource, but for years it's been one of my main motivators. I just wish nearly every change the company made didn't make daily use more frustrating.
Review Summary: User since 2014 who has completed 6 courses at B1/B2 level and dabbled in virtually everything; uses gamification consciously as motivation while also supplementing with other resources; frustrated by ongoing product changes.
Language Learned: Spanish, German, Swedish, French, Portuguese, Italian, Greek, Latin, Ukrainian, Russian, Welsh, others
Key Quote: I just wish nearly every change the company made didn't make daily use more frustrating

Name: Shevyshev
Verbatim Review: Im on around 1500 days maybe? I just can't get rid of my streak. Sometimes I do all of 2 minutes in a day, and my Italian progress has been slow for it. But, it's just for laughs so it all seems good.
Review Summary: ~1500-day streak user who admits he does as little as 2 minutes a day to keep the streak; has made slow Italian progress but treats it as a casual joke with no pressure.
Key Quote: I just can't get rid of my streak

Name: veganx1312
Verbatim Review: I like learning languages in general, and I realized that at least for me it's easier to learn multiple languages at once than just learning one at the time. What I do is using the motivation boost I get when I meet someone that speaks certain language, or when I find out about a book or movie that I consider interesting, or things like that, and I basically kill it in that language as long as I feel like. Once the motivation starts failing, I simply switch to other language. The first Duolingo course I finished was Polish. Then I just switched to 'learning' English from Polish, as if it was my native language. You wouldn't imagine the amount of new words and constructions I learnt by doing that. Right now I'm about to finish the Italian course from Polish, and I'm not native in either of these languages!
Review Summary: Motivation-driven multi-language learner who rides interest spikes in different languages; uses creative cross-language learning (e.g., Italian from Polish) for surprising depth gains.
Key Quote: I basically kill it in that language as long as I feel like

Name: tradegreek
Verbatim Review: I only have a 50~ day streak I'm waiting for my duo super to end I used a one month free code that was given away here and I will move to another app as I don't like the gamification or the guilting you into using it etc it's really good for learning / memorising words but in terms of learning grammar / to speak I don't think it's that good and the cons outweigh the pros for me.
Review Summary: Short 50-day streak user who dislikes the gamification and guilt-loop mechanics; found it useful for vocab but not grammar or speaking; waiting to switch to another app once subscription expires.
Key Quote: the cons outweigh the pros for me

Name: abrady
Verbatim Review: currently just over 1900 days. I just get a little bored doing the same language so I'll alternate especially once I hit a high enough level where Duo seems to really lose its ability to teach well.
Review Summary: 1900-day streak user who rotates languages out of boredom and because Duolingo loses teaching effectiveness at higher levels.
Key Quote: once I hit a high enough level where Duo seems to really lose its ability to teach well

Name: No_Luck3539
Verbatim Review: I'm just about at 2300 in my streak. We have a small resort and were getting quite a few German-speaking guests so thought I'd try to learn some German. Then we were spending time in California in the winter so wanted to learn some Spanish. Now we're expecting a lot of Dutch guests this summer so I'm on to Dutch. I like languages and will have a few more under my belt for an upcoming European river cruise and trip to Costa Rica. Gamification just helps me not skip a day!
Review Summary: ~2300-day streak; business and travel-motivated learner rotating between German, Spanish, and Dutch based on guest demographics and upcoming trips; uses gamification purely as a consistency tool.
Key Quote: Gamification just helps me not skip a day

Name: intratwingled
Verbatim Review: I love learning languages, the more the better. Short attention span perhaps. While I focus on a few of them, I've tried dozens. I found out a few days ago that a co-worker is Greek. Her eyes lit up when I shared the two Greek words I knew. Now I'll spend a few days with Duo learning more. I started watching a Russian TV series with English subtitles, so I studied Russian for several weeks. So much to learn, so little time!
Review Summary: Curiosity-driven learner who follows spontaneous hooks (a Greek coworker, a Russian TV series) to dive into new languages briefly; joyful about the breadth even without depth.
Key Quote: Her eyes lit up when I shared the two Greek words I knew

Name: amyo_b
Verbatim Review: 2500 day streak on mine. I finish trees. And then try to develop the language elsewhere as well. I've finished the Eng to German course (up to 80--they just added new content I haven't started that yet), the German to Spanish course (up to 65 they just added content so I'm doing that right now), the Eng to Dutch course, the Dutch to German course (this one just got new content so I'm working on this one instead of Eng to German), the Spanish to Swedish course and Eng to Finnish course. Currently I'm doing the new material in German to Spanish and Dutch to German, as well as the Eng to Hebrew and Eng to Polish courses. By and large I keep 3-4 active courses at a time. I can use German in my daily life (talking, listening, media consumption etc.), I can enjoy all kinds of content in Dutch and Spanish. I can read Swedish novels. I can read a B1 reader in Finnish (after a whole year with a text book and lots of drilling on Mondly). I can read a A2 reader in Hebrew (I'm at level 26). Polish I'm just starting (level 11). I've done Memrise decks in all of them except Polish. And am currently doing decks in Anki.
Review Summary: 2500-day streak; systematic course completer who runs 3–4 active courses at once and supplements with Memrise, Anki, and textbooks; has achieved daily-life German, media-level Dutch and Spanish, and novel-reading Swedish.
Language Learned: German, Dutch, Spanish, Swedish, Finnish, Hebrew, Polish
Key Quote: I can use German in my daily life (talking, listening, media consumption etc.)

Name: Boris Dorofeev
Verbatim Review: Very fun, feels like a game. I have started Greek, Latin, Spanish, and Music, so far. The reason I give 4 stars isn't because of ads (which are totally fine since the content is still accessible). I give this score because the way this app is structured makes it a supplemental learning tool. Take Latin for example; sure, I have learned some vocabulary. However, there has been nothing on actual grammar which is the actual important part. Nothing on case, mood, etc. Otherwise, very solid app.
Review Summary: Enjoying the gamified format across multiple subjects but frustrated that the app is fundamentally a supplement — especially poor on grammar for Latin.
Key Quote: the way this app is structured makes it a supplemental learning tool

Name: sapphire williams
Verbatim Review: Very easy and simple for me to grasp. Heck even a monkeys uncle can learn a new language through Duolingo, well as long as he has common sense that is. Of the years I've been using this app I've only seen one or two grammar errors, but nothing major. So in all I'll say this app is worth it. Since five stars is considered perfect, I gave four stars because nothing is perfect but can get damn real close.
Review Summary: Long-term user who finds Duolingo easy to grasp and worth it overall; minor grammar errors noted but not dealbreakers.
Key Quote: this app is worth it

Name: MT Nava
Verbatim Review: Awesome free learning program. My big Thankyous! Just one thing, though. It will even help if we get refreshers from the beginning words and phrases of the past learnings incorporated into these ones, so we don't forget them as we advance. Thanks again for allowing me to learn new language for free so I can save money to help my daughter finish school. Duo, you're the best!
Review Summary: Grateful free user who values the no-cost access; requests spaced review of earlier vocabulary to prevent forgetting as lessons advance.
Key Quote: Thanks again for allowing me to learn new language for free

Name: Kaylin Young
Verbatim Review: It's alright, but they honestly only focus on the people that have more subscriptions, if you want more EXP or something like that, you need to buy super, which gives you too many benefits while the people who cannot buy super get but so little rewards. I wish they'd fix the energy bar. I think when we had the hearts, it was much better.
Review Summary: Frustrated by the pay-to-win dynamic and the new energy system; prefers the old hearts system and feels free users are underserved.
Key Quote: the people who cannot buy super get but so little rewards

Name: Behh Thunny
Verbatim Review: Duolingo is most definitely the best language learning app out there! I am currently learning Spanish, and I have over 7k XP in the course! I know how to order food, express feelings, converse about travel, and much more. Its by far the best app you will find for your language learning journey. You can also learn Math, Music, and even Chess! The most surprising part by far would be that you can improve your English skills as an English speaker on the app!
Review Summary: Enthusiastic Spanish learner who has achieved practical skills (ordering food, expressing feelings, travel conversation) and praises the breadth of content including math, music, and chess.
Key Quote: I know how to order food, express feelings, converse about travel, and much more

Name: Brett lomayma
Verbatim Review: Duolingo is a pleasant and easy way to learn a new language, although it can be a bit repetitive at times, although I have found it is also needed. Especially when building new and tonal vocabularies. I would also say that the yearly option is good and keeps you from being discouraged too much when making mistakes and having to start all over.
Review Summary: Finds Duolingo pleasant and repetition useful for tonal vocabulary; recommends the yearly subscription for its forgiveness of mistakes.
Key Quote: it can be a bit repetitive at times, although I have found it is also needed

Name: Lily Alderson
Verbatim Review: I give 4.5 but overall, I think full 5 works better than a 4. This is because: PROS: - Fun, making it quick, easy, and enjoyable to learn - Flexible, giving room to grow at almost every level - Full of choices, like if you want to skip ahead, or pick a language to learn CONS: - It can get a little slow or fast, especially for younger children - Sometimes it skips things or the order it teaches it in dosen't make sense Though me and my family still don't regret getting duolingo.
Review Summary: Family user who appreciates the fun and flexibility; notes pacing can be uneven for younger children and lesson order is sometimes illogical.
Key Quote: me and my family still don't regret getting duolingo

Name: Ana Zuniga
Verbatim Review: It used to be a good app for learning languages, but now it has gone down the toilet! I can't even earn prizes for completing my daily quests because intrusive ads run immediately after. I and you can't get rid of them, and they never end. I wouldn't mind them if they'd end at some point. You are forced to close the app and reopen so you can continue learning. But you lose out on the prize! Also, you can't practice anymore to get your hearts back up. It's made learning more frustrating.
Review Summary: Former satisfied user furious about unending intrusive ads after quest completion and the removal of practice-to-restore-hearts; finds the experience actively hostile now.
Key Quote: it has gone down the toilet

Name: Allison Drake
Verbatim Review: This app used to be a great way to learn a language. You learn at your own pace-as much or as little as you like. Not anymore. The hearts system they used was perfectly fine but they changed it to the energy system-21 pieces of energy. Answer a question,1 piece gone,claim xp at the end of a round another piece gone. Some rounds have 17 questions-21 energy doesn't go far. They give you 2 & 3 for 5 & 10 streaks and 5 for a perfect round. Getting 10 back for 17 qs plus xp claim,18 total-ridiculous.
Review Summary: Detailed breakdown of why the new energy system is mathematically stingier than the old hearts system; feels the change has made learning untenable for free users.
Key Quote: 21 energy doesn't go far

Name: Fiona Siqani
Verbatim Review: Duolingo is a great app! I have been using it for a while, and I have been learning South Korean. I have been learning a lot , and it's making it so easy for me! Even premium is free for a week so u can see how it is! I never tried it, but when u learn a lot, there is a chest, that u can open ., and if you press ur screen a lot, there is a chance that u get Duolingo for three days straight! Thanks Duolingo!!
Review Summary: Enthusiastic Korean learner who finds the app easy and rewarding; excited about the gamified reward mechanics like chests.
Key Quote: I have been learning a lot, and it's making it so easy for me

Name: Vincent Beales
Verbatim Review: I have a love hate relationship with this program. I'm a paid up member after 4 years of use. It can be quirky and frustrating because the English translations are US American and most English speakers don't use the same phraseology. Also some of the voice recognition of Portuguese pronunciation is inconsistent between different types of exercises. I know this from experience living in Brazil 3/4 of every year.
Review Summary: 4-year paid member who lives in Brazil and finds the US-centric English translations and inconsistent Portuguese voice recognition frustrating from a real-world usage standpoint.
Key Quote: I know this from experience living in Brazil 3/4 of every year

Name: Angela
Verbatim Review: Duolingo, this is the best learning app! I have so many learning app, but out of those apps, this is the best learning app. I learn Chinese. I improve a lot in 1 week. I really love duolingo. One thing that for the missions could you add more so that in one day we could do many missions. Because the challenge gave us the badge. But sometimes i did not do the mission. So extra missions please! And sometimes after i finish a lesson the game would just lag so i lose the xp. Pls fix that.
Review Summary: Enthusiastic Chinese learner who improved noticeably in one week; wants more daily missions and reports XP-losing lag bug after lessons.
Key Quote: I improve a lot in 1 week

Name: Solidus
Verbatim Review: You open the app there's an ad. You complate a lesson there's an ad. You try to do another lesson and the app forces you to watch over 5 ads. The recent change to energy forces you to see 5-6 ads for every lesson. This is not a 'free' app If you value your time. Over 60% is just ads. Now I get an ad just by opening the app. I miss the old forums from 2023. They had so much usefull info for learning.
Review Summary: Disgusted by the ad volume — estimates 60%+ of the experience is now ads; energy system compounding the issue; misses the old community forums.
Key Quote: This is not a 'free' app if you value your time

Name: Brian Winter
Verbatim Review: This is a good easy to use App. I did Dutch By Radio in the 70s and I wanted to brush up on my skill. Annoying even though you pay, you still have to buy credits to complete some tasks. Also too many, for my liking annoying things between lessons to add points star and other stuff. Could do with stripping out these annoying distractions. let's just have the lessons.
Review Summary: Older user who studied Dutch decades ago and returned to brush up; finds the gamification distractions annoying and dislikes needing to buy credits even as a paying member.
Key Quote: Could do with stripping out these annoying distractions. let's just have the lessons

Name: Dood13 N00d13 (D00M3D1D10T)
Verbatim Review: in all honesty I've been using this app as well as others while taking japanese in person for the last three years, sure it helps me keep up with studying while in more advanced courses for hs but over all shouldn't be used singularly for beginner since I do belive it's better to learn and understand how hiragana and katakana should work first then as more words are applied move more into Kanji.
Review Summary: High school Japanese student using Duolingo alongside in-person classes for 3 years; advises against using it as sole tool for beginners since it skips script fundamentals.
Key Quote: shouldn't be used singularly for beginner

Name: George Khachadourian
Verbatim Review: I'm thoroughly enjoying the process of acquiring a new language via Duolingo. However, I kindly request the inclusion of grammatical explanations. This would help us comprehend, for instance, why a particular verb is structured in a certain manner within one sentence, yet takes on an alternative form in another.
Review Summary: Enjoying the experience but requests grammar explanations to understand verb conjugation rules rather than just encountering them in context.
Key Quote: I kindly request the inclusion of grammatical explanations

Name: Ena Abodakpi
Verbatim Review: I really like Duolingo but there's one thing I do not like. energy. The energy doesn't give me or anyone else time to learn and we lose out on xp and time. On the app it tells us to not quit a lesson but if we do not have time or gems we cannot do what the app is for, learning. I would rate it 4 Stars if energy was deleted.
Review Summary: Likes the app but the energy system directly contradicts the app's own advice not to quit lessons — runs out of energy before being able to complete learning goals.
Key Quote: the energy doesn't give me or anyone else time to learn

Name: K S
Verbatim Review: Doesn't recognise voice input. I started using my tablet because voice input wasn't recognised on my phone. It worked fine on my tablet until I updated. Now, whatever I speak isn't recognised making the app pretty useless - knowing words isn't enough, to get by in a language you have to be able to SPEAK IT, if your voice isn't recognised then you'll never know if you're speaking the right words.
Review Summary: Voice recognition failure across multiple devices after an update; frames it as a fundamental gap — you need to speak a language to use it, not just read it.
Key Quote: to get by in a language you have to be able to SPEAK IT

Name: A RosieGlow
Verbatim Review: I'm so mad at the greediness Duolingo has turned to. You have to buy superduolingo to get a decent experience on the app. If you don't pay, there is an ad after every lesson. It's extremely annoying. And if you don't pay, when you finish just a couple of lessons, it's like oh watch an ad to get diamonds to play a lesson, like dude you're wasting my time making me watch ads and doing very little of the LEARNING. Very disruptive experience, way too many Ads.
Review Summary: Angry at Duolingo's monetization trajectory; feels the free experience is now deliberately degraded to push subscription purchase.
Key Quote: You have to buy superduolingo to get a decent experience on the app

Name: Mickey Moose
Verbatim Review: VERY FRUSTRATING! Each lesson begins by throwing you into the deep end - new words appear without any notice or translation, forcing the student to guess at its meaning. Same goes for translating the writing - most times you have to keep guessing, and if you get it wrong, the lesson makes you do it over and over until you get it right. If this were an in-person college course, I would drop it!
Review Summary: Deeply frustrated by the trial-and-error teaching method — new words appear without introduction, forcing guessing; compares negatively to a formal classroom standard.
Key Quote: If this were an in-person college course, I would drop it

Name: fim osmani
Verbatim Review: good way to learn if they let me do as many lessons as I want. but each question drains energy and if you run out you can't do anymore lessons. I tried learning Japanese but it just stars teaching you words without kana. it makes it so that kana is optional and isn't as important. i did learn some japense though. I wish that they teach kana first THEN words and stuff. also let me do as much lessons as I want.
Review Summary: Wants unlimited lesson access but blocked by energy drain; also frustrated that Japanese course skips kana fundamentals and goes straight to vocabulary.
Key Quote: each question drains energy and if you run out you can't do anymore lessons

Name: Amjed Ali
Verbatim Review: I am learning norwegion, I am just 11 years old now,when i was 8, i instslled duollingo,thinking that it's a game, after playing and learning english, I start speaking english very good,excellent. I also learned so many difficult and hard words! And now, look at me, I won so many prises in english speech competition, I REALLY THANKS TO DUOLINGO!!! It is the best language learning app,I love it. My aunt is from norway,That's why i am learning norwegion,And also for fun!!!
Review Summary: 11-year-old who downloaded Duolingo at age 8 thinking it was a game; credits it for his English fluency and speech competition wins; now learning Norwegian for family and fun.
Key Quote: I won so many prises in english speech competition, I REALLY THANKS TO DUOLINGO

Name: Tracey Hanwell
Verbatim Review: Great for reviewing a language you studied in school. Pretty good for learning a new language. I think for a new language students should get a textbook and use the book and Duolingo together. This app used to work well on the free side. Watching commercials is a fair exchange. But the recent restrictions of only allowing 3 mistakes a day is a HORRIBLE feature. Who thought punishing students for mistakes is a good idea? Why not limit how many lessons can be completed, instead?
Review Summary: Recommends pairing with a textbook; satisfied with review use but furious at the new mistake-limit system — frames punishing errors as pedagogically wrong.
Key Quote: Who thought punishing students for mistakes is a good idea?

Name: Eren
Verbatim Review: an app designed to take a lifetime so that you keep paying instead of actually learning. Duolingo is one of the worst apps to learn a language even as a supplement, you won't speak the language after 3 years of duolingo. Just an overpriced, overgamified, overmarketed scam.
Review Summary: Dismisses Duolingo as a deliberately slow, overgamified scam designed to extract money rather than teach languages; claims no speaking ability develops even after years.
Key Quote: you won't speak the language after 3 years of duolingo

Name: Emily Pasquini
Verbatim Review: its nice, but its very annoying, the heart method was much better then energy, because now to do more lessons I need watch a bunch of ads, I only use it for chess anyway, because its the only lessons that are a bit useful, I used to do french but it got very frustrating, new words popped out of nowhere with no grammar explanation, just examples, and when the new words come out of nowhere its hard to understand what it means or what that word is. 2 stars only because of chess, it wouldve been 1.
Review Summary: Gave up on French due to lack of grammar explanations and now only uses the app for chess; frustrated by energy system and ad burden; would have rated 1 star without chess.
Key Quote: I used to do french but it got very frustrating

Name: Alice
Verbatim Review: the feature to do speaking doesn't work properly, I was trying to learn French to get a leg up in that class, but there were quite a few words that no matter how hard I tried, it kept marking it wrong, so I googled it and had the official Google voice read it out, and you'd think it would definitely mark it right but no! it was still wrong! then I wanted to investigate so I installed duo on another device set to learn English (my only language!) and it still marked my pronunciation wrong! Scam!
Review Summary: Tested broken voice recognition by having Google TTS read French words — still marked wrong; calls it a scam after the same bug failed even English pronunciation.
Key Quote: I had the official Google voice read it out, and you'd think it would definitely mark it right but no!

Name: Ameya - Jeremy
Verbatim Review: Duolingo is the most effective learning app l've ever used. It's taught me spanish,french,and german really well, and taught me more than l knew in chess. But there is an issue. The music notes aren't on the lines they are in real life, and l feel that's wrong. That's it for me, 'cause you fixed my other complaints, thank you!
Review Summary: High praise — credits Duolingo for teaching Spanish, French, and German well plus chess; single remaining complaint is a music notation display error.
Key Quote: It's taught me spanish, french, and german really well

Name: Kirsty McInroy
Verbatim Review: I really enjoy duolingo as I have Scottish family and it's nice to learn their language(Scottish Gaelic which is less spoken). The only thing is the ads and the energy you have on it you start with 25 but it goes down every question. And I'm trying to get a lot of lessons done but I keep running out of energy!
Review Summary: Learning Scottish Gaelic for family connection; enjoying the content but energy depletion is cutting sessions short before goals are met.
Key Quote: I keep running out of energy

Name: Ayyub Aslam Vakkayil
Verbatim Review: I love this app!!!!!This app has alot of languages you can learn and even chess, maths and some other stuff!I love it even though it has alot of in-app ads...but the ads are pretty funny(some),the only two reasons it doesn't feel right are because 1.The lessons keep repeating the same thing so I can just memorize the question and answer,and 2.For the hard lessons,it repeats another easy lesson(usually the one right before it).
Review Summary: Enthusiastic about the content variety; notes two structural flaws — repetition allows question/answer memorization rather than real learning, and difficulty progression is poorly calibrated.
Key Quote: The lessons keep repeating the same thing so I can just memorize the question and answer

Name: Astro
Verbatim Review: the best language app ever!! its so fun! edit after one year: its still very fun, but once you lose the streak you get so unmotivated that you dont use the app for days. it also displays super and max alot of times, nearly after every lesson. even if you buy super just to avoid the ads it will still find ways to make you want max.
Review Summary: Still enjoys the app after a year but flags streak loss as demotivating enough to cause multi-day dropout; also frustrated by upsell pressure for Max even after paying for Super.
Key Quote: once you lose the streak you get so unmotivated that you dont use the app for days

Name: William Johnson
Verbatim Review: Doulingo was fantistic for the first 6 months leaning Japanese. I learned a lot and fast and hit lvl 23. I paid for a 1 year subsciption and was very happy with it. However this changed overnite. They have added spelling to the lessons, one was Mapo Tofu. Any input on the tablet only brought up hirigana charactors, instant fail. Worst yet you must complete the spelling to finish the lesson. This app went from a fun learning app to a miserable experiance. No longer buying subscritions.

Key Quote: This app went from a fun learning app to a miserable experience

Name: Anaya Nana
Verbatim Review: I DO NOT understand HOW this is the 1# language learning app. it is NOT a good language learning app for people who dont pay. first of all... you barely talk! you need to pay for a phone call talk! a PROPER language learning app should let their people talk! YOU NEED TO TALK TO BE ABLE TO SPEAK THE LANGUAGE! second of all.. To have a fun experience... you need to PAY!? people should be having fun without needing to pay!
Review Summary: Furious that speaking practice is gated behind payment and the free experience is too limited to develop actual language ability — questions how it can be rated the top app.

Name: Nicole
Verbatim Review: this is the best language learning app ever! I would be so happy If you could add more languages, because I really want to learn Latvian, but I honestly cannot find a better app than doulingo to learn Latvian, doulingo literally makes me remember languages so easy!! so please add Latvian if you can.
Review Summary: Enthusiastic user who finds the app highly effective for language retention; frustrated only by missing Latvian in the catalog.

Name: Keoleam Chantika
Verbatim Review: I like the app bc it makes my grammar get better in english so I'll rate it a 2 stars. bc I really hated it when I did it perfectly but I kept losing energy.it's just bc when after I finish a lesson, an ads pop and the last word that says try ur free trial today and bc it wants me to put the premium one. also the max thing is that I wanna quit on Duo but I'm in a high streak I kept doing it so.
Review Summary: Acknowledges grammar improvement but rates it 2 stars due to energy loss on perfect lessons and aggressive premium upsells; explicitly says streak is the only reason she hasn't quit.
